stem: A box contains [[r]] red chips and [[b]] blue chips, all identical apart from color. One chip is drawn at random, each chip equally likely. What is the probability that the chip drawn is red?
solution: There are \( [[r]] + [[b]] \) equally likely chips and [[r]] of them are red, so the probability is \( \frac{\text{favorable}}{\text{total}} = \frac{[[r]]}{[[r]] + [[b]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the probability of drawing a blue chip, the complementary event.
This divides favorable outcomes by unfavorable outcomes instead of by the total.
This inverts the ratio, dividing blue by red.
This overcounts the favorable red chips by one.
This undercounts the favorable red chips by one.
This divides the total by the favorable count, inverting the probability.

stem: Two fair [[d]]-sided dice, each numbered 1 through [[d]], are rolled once. All \( [[d]]^2 \) outcomes (ordered pairs of results) are equally likely. What is the probability that the two dice show a sum of exactly [[s]]?
solution: The ordered pairs \( (i, j) \) with \( i + j = [[s]] \) and \( 1 \le i, j \le [[d]] \) are \( (1, [[s]]-1), (2, [[s]]-2), \dots, ([[s]]-1, 1) \), giving \( [[s]] - 1 \) favorable outcomes out of \( [[d]]^2 \) total: \( \frac{[[s]] - 1}{[[d]]^2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This off-by-one counts [[s]] favorable pairs instead of \( [[s]] - 1 \).
This divides by [[d]] instead of \( [[d]]^2 \), forgetting the second die.
This treats only one specific pair as favorable, ignoring all the other ways to reach the sum.
This uses the favorable-pair count for the complementary high-sum case instead of sum [[s]].
This undercounts the favorable pairs by one, using \( [[s]] - 2 \).
This overcounts the favorable pairs by one, using \( [[s]] + 1 \).

stem: A bag contains [[r]] red marbles and [[b]] blue marbles. Two marbles are drawn at once at random, with every pair of marbles equally likely to be chosen. What is the probability that BOTH marbles drawn are red?
solution: There are \( \binom{[[r]] + [[b]]}{2} \) equally likely pairs of marbles in total, and \( \binom{[[r]]}{2} \) of them consist of two red marbles: \( \dfrac{\binom{[[r]]}{2}}{\binom{[[r]] + [[b]]}{2}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This treats the two draws as independent WITH replacement, squaring the single-draw probability instead of counting pairs without replacement.
This divides by the number of all-blue pairs instead of by the total number of pairs.
This forgets to divide by 2 for the unordered pair, effectively double-counting order.
This divides by the square of the total count instead of the number of unordered pairs \( \binom{[[r]]+[[b]]}{2} \).
This is just the probability a single marble drawn is red, ignoring that two must both be red.
This inverts the ratio of favorable to total pairs.

stem: Two fair [[d]]-sided dice, each numbered 1 through [[d]], are rolled once, one designated the 'first' die and the other the 'second'. All \( [[d]]^2 \) outcomes are equally likely. What is the probability that the first die shows a STRICTLY GREATER number than the second die?
solution: There are \( \binom{[[d]]}{2} \) unordered pairs of distinct values, and for each, exactly one of the two orderings has the first die strictly greater; ties (equal values) are excluded. So the favorable count is \( \binom{[[d]]}{2} \) out of \( [[d]]^2 \) total outcomes: \( \dfrac{\binom{[[d]]}{2}}{[[d]]^2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This naively assumes greater-than and less-than are each exactly half, ignoring that ties are also possible (this is only exactly true in the limit, not for finite [[d]]).
This forgets to square [[d]] in the denominator, leaving the count of outcomes for only one die.
This wrongly includes ties as part of the 'strictly greater' favorable outcomes.
This mistakenly adds a tie count (a spurious extra [[d]]) to the numerator.
This is the probability the two dice show the same number (a tie), not that the first exceeds the second.
This is the complementary probability (first die less than or equal to the second).
